message:
Fix CRLF line continuations and reorder patterns to prevent false positives

- src/packs/mod.rs:
  - Update  to handle  escapes correctly
  - Restore  check in  to prevent over-normalization of safe args
  - Fix doc comments
- src/normalize.rs: Update test expectation for -S splitting support
- tests/repro_line_continuation_split.rs: Add regression test for CRLF line continuation
- src/packs/search/*.rs: Reorder destructive patterns to prioritize specific sub-resources (doc, close, delete_by_query) before generic index deletion
- src/packs/messaging/sqs_sns.rs: Reorder patterns to prioritize  over
- tests/pattern_audit.rs: Update backtracking audit baseline
- tests/allowlist_command_tests.rs: Fix clippy format string lint
- tests/repro_env_parsing.rs: Fix unused import lint
- src/evaluator.rs: Allow  lint for complex logic function

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-1gv","title":"[PACK] remote.ssh - SSH Pack","description":"# SSH Remote Access Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for SSH operations, particularly remote command execution.

## Destructive Patterns
### Remote Command Execution
- `ssh host 'dangerous-command'` - Detect destructive commands executed remotely
- Piping destructive scripts through SSH
- Redirecting scripts into SSH

### Key Management
- `ssh-keygen -R host` - Removes known hosts (security implication)
- Deleting SSH keys

### Config Operations
- Modifying ~/.ssh/config destructively
- Removing authorized_keys entries

## Safe Patterns
- `ssh -V` (version)
- `ssh-keygen -l` (fingerprint display)
- `ssh-keyscan` (key scanning)
- `ssh host` (interactive session - user takes responsibility)
- `ssh host 'safe-command'` (non-destructive remote commands)

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: remote.* (git_safety_guard-g5o)
- Keywords: ssh, ssh-keygen
- CRITICAL: Must detect when OTHER destructive commands are tunneled through SSH

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests for remote delete commands via SSH
- [ ] Unit tests for piped script detection
- [ ] Unit tests for key management operations
- [ ] E2E test: `scripts/e2e_tests/remote_ssh.txt`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Remote destructive commands detected
- [ ] Key removal operations blocked
- [ ] Safe SSH usage allowed

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-26f","title":"Define TypeScript destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define TypeScript heredoc patterns with a focus on **runtime destructive operations**, while avoiding low-signal “TypeScript style” heuristics that would create false positives.

## Scope boundary

TypeScript compiles to JavaScript; destructive operations are the same at runtime.

Therefore:

- Reuse the JavaScript/Node pattern families from `git_safety_guard-mcf`.
- Add only TypeScript-syntax coverage (types, generics, decorators) so the parser can still match the same runtime calls.

## Pattern families (v1)

- Recursive filesystem deletion:
  - `fs.rmSync(..., { recursive: true })`
  - `fs.rmdirSync(..., { recursive: true })`
- Process execution with destructive payload:
  - `child_process.execSync("rm -rf ...")`
  - `spawnSync("rm", ["-rf", ...])`

## Explicitly out of scope (avoid FPs)

- Heuristics like “@ts-ignore preceding dangerous code”
- “any type casts” / “type assertions” as signals

These are too common and do not map directly to destructive operations.

## Acceptance Criteria

- TS patterns are a superset of JS patterns in terms of runtime destructive operations, differing only in syntax support.
- Includes positive + negative fixtures that exercise TS syntax (types/generics/decorators) without changing runtime behavior.
- No default hard denies unless the matched operation is clearly catastrophic.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-2cu","title":"Design false positive reduction strategy for string arguments","description":"## Objective

Design and implement strategies to dramatically reduce false positives in command blocking, especially for documentation and string arguments.

## The Core Problem

The current regex-based guard suffers from context blindness. It sees:
```
bd create --description="Pattern blocks rm -rf commands"
```

And matches "rm -rf" without understanding it's:
1. Inside a quoted string
2. An argument to a documentation tool
3. NOT actually being executed

This creates severe workflow disruption when trying to document the very patterns we're blocking.

## Why This Is Critical

False positives are arguably WORSE than false negatives:
- False negative: A dangerous command might slip through (rare, recoverable with backups)
- False positive: Blocks legitimate work, forces workarounds, erodes trust in the tool
- Repeated false positives lead users to disable the guard entirely

The guard must be TRUSTED to make intelligent decisions, not blindly pattern-match text.

## Solution Approaches

### 1. Command Structure Analysis (Primary Approach)

Parse the top-level command with a lightweight shell/token model to understand structure:
- Identify command name (bd, git, echo, etc.)
- Identify argument positions (which are options vs values)
- Identify quoting context (single, double, unquoted)
- Only apply destructive patterns to EXECUTABLE positions

Example analysis:
```
bd create --title="..." --description="rm -rf pattern docs"
         ^command       ^option         ^string value (NOT executed)
```

### 2. Safe Command Registry

Maintain a list of commands that take non-executable string arguments:
- bd create, bd update (--description, --title)
- git commit (-m), git tag (-m)
- echo, printf (arguments are printed, not executed)
- grep, rg (pattern arguments)

For these commands, don't apply destructive patterns to their string arguments.

### 3. Execution Context Detection

Distinguish between:
- **Direct execution**: The string IS the command (`bash dangerous_cmd`)
- **String literal**: The string is DATA passed to a command (`bd --desc="..."`)
- **Heredoc body**: Requires language-specific analysis (the whole point of ast-grep)

### 4. Two-Phase Analysis

1. **Quick structural check**: parse command structure, identify context
2. **Pattern matching**: only apply patterns to executable contexts
3. **Deep analysis**: for heredocs/complex cases, use ast-grep / language-aware scanning

### 5. Confidence Scoring (optional later)

Instead of binary block/allow:
- High confidence dangerous: block immediately
- Medium confidence: more thorough analysis
- Low confidence (looks like documentation): allow with optional warning

## Implementation Strategy

1. Choose the minimal parsing substrate that meets performance needs:
   - tokenization + quoting + operator detection (pipes/subst)
   - optionally tree-sitter-bash if needed for correctness
2. Define `ExecutionContext` enum: Direct, StringArg, Heredoc, PipeTarget, CommandSubst
3. Maintain a Safe String-Argument Registry (command + flags) and apply it only in `StringArg` context.
4. Ensure ambiguity defaults to safe behavior (do not “trust” unknown flags as safe).

## Test Cases

Essential false positive tests:
- `bd create --description="This blocks rm -rf"` → ALLOW
- `git commit -m "Fix rm -rf pattern matching"` → ALLOW
- `echo "example: git reset --hard"` → ALLOW
- `grep "rm -rf" patterns.txt` → ALLOW

Essential true positive tests (should still block):
- `rm -rf /tmp/*` → BLOCK
- `bash -c "rm -rf /"` → BLOCK
- `python3 << 'EOF'
  import os; os.system("rm -rf /")
  EOF` → BLOCK (via heredoc analysis)

## Acceptance Criteria

- Design is captured in a self-contained doc/ADR-like section (does not require reading external plan docs).
- Defines:
  - the execution-context model
  - the initial Safe String-Argument Registry scope
  - conservative default rules for ambiguity
- Provides a concrete test matrix of:
  - must-allow doc/test cases
  - must-block executed cases
- Specifies a performance budget for the parsing/classification layer (initial target: sub-5ms worst-case, revise after baseline).

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-2d4","title":"Define Perl destructive patterns for heredoc scanning","description":"## Objective

Define Perl heredoc matchers that detect **high-signal destructive operations** while minimizing false positives.

## Scope boundary

- Focus on irreversible filesystem/process operations.
- Avoid broad “Perl is scary” patterns (eval/regex/taint/network) as default denies; they are not directly destructive and will produce noise.

## Pattern families (v1)

### 1) Process execution with destructive payload

- `system("rm -rf ...")` (literal)
- backticks / `qx//` containing literal destructive payload
- `exec("git reset --hard ...")` (literal)

### 2) Recursive deletion / filesystem destructive ops

- `File::Path::rmtree($path)` (literal catastrophic target → Error; otherwise Warning)
- `unlink($path)` / `rmdir($path)` (Warning by default unless catastrophic target)

## False positive mitigation

- Do not flag `system($VAR)` as deny.
- Do not flag module imports alone.
- Require negative fixtures that include documentation strings and harmless cleanup.

## Acceptance Criteria

- Defines a Perl pattern set aligned with severity taxonomy and focused on destructive outcomes.
- Includes at least 5 positive and 5 negative fixtures.
- No default hard denies unless the matched operation is clearly catastrophic.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-7z4","title":"[PACK] apigateway.kong - Kong API Gateway Pack","description":"# Kong API Gateway Pack

## Scope
Implement pattern matching for Kong Gateway CLI and API operations.

## Destructive Patterns
### kong CLI
- `kong delete services`
- `kong delete routes`
- `kong delete plugins`
- `kong delete consumers`
- `kong delete upstreams`
- `kong delete targets`

### deck CLI (declarative config)
- `deck reset` - DANGEROUS! Removes all config
- `deck sync --select-tag X` (with destructive intent)

### Admin API
- `curl -X DELETE :8001/services/`
- `curl -X DELETE :8001/routes/`

## Safe Patterns
- `kong get services`
- `kong list routes`
- `deck dump`
- `deck diff`
- `deck ping`
- GET requests to Admin API

## Implementation Notes
- Parent category: apigateway.* (git_safety_guard-5qa)
- Keywords: kong, deck
- CRITICAL: `deck reset` is extremely dangerous

## Testing Requirements
- [ ] Unit tests for service/route deletion
- [ ] Unit tests for `deck reset` detection
- [ ] Unit tests for Admin API patterns

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-7zg","title":"Add UBS installation and run steps to CI workflow","description":"# Task: Add UBS Installation and Run Steps to CI

## What to Do

Add two steps to the check job in .github/workflows/ci.yml:
1. Install UBS using the official installer
2. Run UBS on changed Rust files

## CI Steps to Add

After the clippy step, add:

```yaml
- name: Install UBS
  run: |
    curl -fsSL "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Dicklesworthstone/ultimate_bug_scanner/master/install.sh" \
      | bash -s -- --easy-mode
    # Add UBS to PATH for this job
    echo "$HOME/.ubs" >> $GITHUB_PATH

- name: Run UBS on changed files
  if: always()
  run: |
    echo "=== UBS Static Analysis ===" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "Timestamp: $(date -Iseconds)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    
    # Get changed Rust files
    if [ "${{ github.event_name }}" = "pull_request" ]; then
      CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only origin/${{ github.base_ref }}...HEAD -- "*.rs" | tr "\n" " ")
      echo "Mode: Pull Request (base: ${{ github.base_ref }})" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    else
      CHANGED=$(git diff --name-only HEAD~1 -- "*.rs" | tr "\n" " ")
      echo "Mode: Push (comparing to HEAD~1)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    fi
    
    echo "Changed files: ${CHANGED:-none}" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    
    if [ -n "$CHANGED" ]; then
      echo "Running UBS analysis..." | tee -a ubs-output.log
      # Run UBS with verbose output
      if ubs --verbose $CHANGED 2>&1 | tee -a ubs-output.log; then
        echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
        echo "UBS Result: PASSED (no issues found)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
      else
        echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
        echo "UBS Result: WARNINGS (issues found - non-blocking)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
        echo "::warning::UBS found potential issues in changed files"
      fi
    else
      echo "UBS Result: SKIPPED (no Rust files changed)" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    fi
    
    # Summary
    echo "" | tee -a ubs-output.log
    echo "=== End UBS Analysis ===" | tee -a ubs-output.log

- name: UBS summary
  if: always()
  run: |
    echo "## UBS Static Analysis" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    cat ubs-output.log >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY 2>/dev/null || echo "No UBS output" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
    echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

- name: Upload UBS output
  if: failure()
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: ubs-output
    path: ubs-output.log
    retention-days: 7
```

## Key Fixes from Original

1. **Binary Path**: Added `echo "$HOME/.ubs" >> $GITHUB_PATH` to make ubs available
2. **Detailed Logging**: All output goes to ubs-output.log with timestamps
3. **Clear Status**: Explicit PASSED/WARNINGS/SKIPPED messages
4. **Artifact Upload**: Save output on failure for debugging
5. **Step Summary**: UBS results appear in GitHub Actions summary

## Why --verbose

The verbose flag provides:
- File-by-file analysis progress
- Detailed issue descriptions
- Context around found issues

## Why Warning Mode

Using warning annotations instead of failing because:
- UBS is new to this repo, may have false positives
- Dont want to block PRs unexpectedly
- Can change to blocking after tuning

## Files to Modify

- .github/workflows/ci.yml (check job)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] UBS install step added with PATH setup
- [ ] UBS run step added with detailed logging
- [ ] Changed-files logic works for PRs and pushes
- [ ] ubs-output.log captures all analysis output
- [ ] Step summary shows UBS results
- [ ] Artifacts uploaded on failure
- [ ] CI passes with new steps","notes":"Testing/Validation:

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-8u1","title":"[DESIGN] Architectural Decisions for Pack Implementation","description":"# Architectural Decisions for Pack Implementation

## Overview
Before implementing new packs, several architectural decisions need to be documented and agreed upon. These decisions affect all packs and should be resolved BEFORE implementation begins.

## Decisions to Document

### 1. REST API Pattern Detection Strategy
**Problem**: Many tools use REST APIs via curl/httpie. How do we detect `curl -X DELETE api.example.com/resource`?

**Options**:
A. Keyword-based: Match `curl` + detect DELETE method + known API hostnames
B. AST-based: Parse curl arguments to extract method and URL
C. Hybrid: Quick keyword check, then AST parse for suspected matches

**Considerations**:
- Performance impact of AST parsing
- False positive rate for each approach
- Maintainability of hostname lists

### 2. Pack Overlap Resolution
**Problem**: cicd.github_actions and platform.github both cover `gh` commands

**Options**:
A. Strict separation: Each pack handles distinct commands
B. Layered: Platform pack is base, cicd pack extends
C. Merged: Single github pack with all patterns

**Considerations**:
- User configuration complexity
- Pattern duplication
- Maintenance burden

### 3. Command Alias Handling
**Problem**: `kubectl` vs `k`, `docker` vs `podman`, etc.

**Options**:
A. Explicit aliases: List all known aliases per pack
B. Keyword expansion: User configures their aliases
C. Pattern-based: Use regex to catch common alias patterns

**Considerations**:
- User customization needs
- Performance of expanded keyword lists

### 4. Performance Budget Per Pack
**Problem**: Each pack adds patterns. How do we prevent slowdown?

**Proposal**:
- Maximum patterns per pack: 50
- Maximum regex complexity score: TBD
- Benchmark requirement: <1ms per pack evaluation

### 5. Safe vs Destructive Threshold
**Problem**: Some commands are context-dependent (e.g., `rsync` is safe without --delete)

**Options**:
A. Flag-aware matching: Parse flags to determine safety
B. Conservative: Block ambiguous commands
C. Configurable: User sets their preference

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Each decision documented with chosen option and rationale
- [ ] Performance benchmarks established
- [ ] Design doc created in docs/architecture/pack-design.md
- [ ] Team review completed

## Dependencies
- Should be resolved BEFORE implementing Tier 1 packs
- Informs testing strategy","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:09:53.183447098-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-10T01:51:09.662426829-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-10T01:51:09.662426829-05:00","close_reason":"Documented pack architecture decisions in docs/architecture/pack-design.md; pending team review","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-8u1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ltou","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:35:21.471784408-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-8u1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-ikf2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:35:21.510516334-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"},{"issue_id":"git_safety_guard-8u1","depends_on_id":"git_safety_guard-qdhh","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-09T15:35:21.550636035-05:00","created_by":"ubuntu"}]}
 {"id":"git_safety_guard-8wt","title":"Create comprehensive test suite for heredoc detection","description":"## Objective

Create comprehensive test coverage for heredoc detection and AST-based pattern matching, including unit tests, integration tests, and bypass attempt tests.

## Test Categories

### 1. Unit Tests

#### Heredoc Detection Tests

- Bash heredoc variants: `<<EOF`, `<<-EOF`, `<<<`, `<<'EOF'`, `<<"EOF"`
- Python inline scripts: `python3 -c ...`
- Node.js: `node -e ...`, `node <<EOF`
- Perl: `perl -e ...`, `perl <<'END'`
- Ruby: `ruby -e ...`, heredoc variants

#### Language Detection Tests

- Detection from command prefix (python3, node, ruby, perl, bash)
- Detection from shebang (`#!/usr/bin/env python3`)
- Detection from content heuristics (bounded)
- Fallback behavior for unknown languages

#### Pattern Matching Tests

- Each destructive pattern per language (high-signal)
- Each safe/negative pattern (things that look dangerous but aren't)
- Edge cases in quoting and escaping

### 2. Integration Tests

#### Pipeline Tests

- Full flow from JSON input to block/allow decision
- Heredoc commands correctly trigger deep analysis
- Non-heredoc commands still use fast path
- Performance within latency budget

#### Error Handling Tests

- Malformed heredoc syntax
- Unparseable content
- Unknown languages fall back gracefully
- Timeout handling (fail-open in hook mode)

### 3. Bypass Attempt Tests

Curate a set of realistic bypass attempts we want to cover, e.g.:

- variable indirection (`CMD=rm; $CMD -rf ...`)
- command substitution (`$(echo rm) -rf ...`)
- `bash -c` nesting

(These should be weighted toward high-signal, common bypasses; avoid building a giant speculative list that creates noisy tests.)

### 4. False Positive Tests

Ensure we DON'T block legitimate uses:

- documentation containing command examples
- grep/rg patterns searching for dangerous commands
- `bd create` descriptions that mention dangerous patterns

### 5. Performance Tests

- Tier 1 trigger latency
- extraction latency
- AST match latency (bounded)
- large heredoc handling (size limits)

## Test infrastructure sketch

```
tests/
  fixtures/
    heredocs/
    bypass_attempts/
    false_positives/
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- Test suite is structured so failures are easy to attribute (tier1/extract/lang/match).
- Includes a curated corpus that covers:
  - main heredoc forms
  - at least one example per supported language
  - a small set of realistic bypass attempts
  - a strong false-positive regression set
- Enforces policy behaviors:
  - hook fail-open on parse/timeout
  - severity mapping (deny vs warn)
- Provides clear logs/assert messages so CI failures are actionable.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-8zo","title":"Add memory tests job to CI workflow","description":"# Task: Add Memory Tests Job to CI Workflow

## What to Do

Add a dedicated CI job for running memory leak tests with comprehensive logging and artifact collection.

## Changes to .github/workflows/ci.yml

Add new job after the coverage job:

```yaml
  # Memory leak detection tests
  memory-tests:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    needs: check
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@nightly

      - name: Cache cargo registry and target
        uses: actions/cache@v4
        with:
          path: |
            ~/.cargo/registry
            ~/.cargo/git
            target
          key: \${{ runner.os }}-cargo-memory-\${{ hashFiles(\"**/Cargo.lock\", \"**/Cargo.toml\") }}
          restore-keys: |
            \${{ runner.os }}-cargo-memory-

      - name: Run memory tests
        id: memory_tests
        run: |
          echo "=== DCG Memory Leak Tests ===" | tee memory-output.log
          echo "Timestamp: $(date -Iseconds)" | tee -a memory-output.log
          echo "Runner: ${{ runner.os }}" | tee -a memory-output.log
          echo "Rust: $(rustc --version)" | tee -a memory-output.log
          echo "" | tee -a memory-output.log
          
          # Get baseline system memory
          echo "=== System Memory Baseline ===" | tee -a memory-output.log
          free -h | tee -a memory-output.log
          echo "" | tee -a memory-output.log
          
          echo "=== Running Memory Tests ===" | tee -a memory-output.log
          # Memory tests must run sequentially for accurate measurements
          # Release mode for realistic performance characteristics
          if cargo test --test memory_tests --release -- --nocapture --test-threads=1 2>&1 | tee -a memory-output.log; then
            echo "" | tee -a memory-output.log
            echo "=== Memory Tests: ALL PASSED ===" | tee -a memory-output.log
            echo "memory_tests_result=passed" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
          else
            echo "" | tee -a memory-output.log
            echo "=== Memory Tests: FAILED ===" | tee -a memory-output.log
            echo "memory_tests_result=failed" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
            exit 1
          fi

      - name: Parse memory metrics
        if: always()
        run: |
          echo "=== Memory Test Metrics ===" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          echo "" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          
          # Extract metrics from test output
          echo "Test Results:" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          grep -E "^memory_" memory-output.log | tee -a memory-metrics.log || echo "No metrics found" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          
          echo "" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          echo "Growth Summary:" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          grep -E "final.*growth" memory-output.log | tee -a memory-metrics.log || echo "No growth data" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          
          echo "" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          echo "Pass/Fail:" | tee -a memory-metrics.log
          grep -E "(PASSED|FAILED|panicked)" memory-output.log | tee -a memory-metrics.log || echo "No status found" | tee -a memory-metrics.log

      - name: Memory test summary
        if: always()
        run: |
          echo "## Memory Leak Tests" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          
          # Result badge
          if grep -q "ALL PASSED" memory-output.log; then
            echo "**Result:** ✅ All tests passed" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          else
            echo "**Result:** ❌ Tests failed" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          fi
          echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          
          # Metrics table
          echo "### Memory Growth by Test" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "| Test | Final Growth | Limit | Status |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "|------|--------------|-------|--------|" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          
          # Parse and format metrics
          grep -E "final.*growth" memory-output.log | while read line; do
            test_name=$(echo "$line" | grep -oP "^[^:]+")
            growth=$(echo "$line" | grep -oP "growth: \K[0-9]+ KB" || echo "?")
            limit=$(echo "$line" | grep -oP "limit: \K[0-9]+ KB" || echo "?")
            if echo "$line" | grep -q "PASSED"; then
              status="✅"
            else
              status="❌"
            fi
            echo "| $test_name | $growth | $limit | $status |" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          done
          
          echo "" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "### Full Output" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          tail -50 memory-output.log >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY
          echo "\`\`\`" >> $GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY

      - name: Upload memory test artifacts
        if: always()
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
        with:
          name: memory-test-output
          path: |
            memory-output.log
            memory-metrics.log
          retention-days: 14
```

## Why This Design

### Separate Job
1. **Isolation** - Memory measurements need dedicated resources
2. **Sequential** - --test-threads=1 required for accurate measurements
3. **Release mode** - More realistic than debug builds
4. **Different cache** - Separate cache key for memory builds

### Comprehensive Logging
1. **Timestamped output** - Know when tests ran
2. **System baseline** - Memory state before tests
3. **Per-test metrics** - Growth tracked per test
4. **Clear status** - PASSED/FAILED for each test

### Artifact Collection
1. **Always upload** - Even on success (for trend analysis)
2. **14-day retention** - Long enough to debug regressions
3. **Multiple files** - Raw output + parsed metrics

### Step Summary
1. **Result badge** - Quick pass/fail visibility
2. **Metrics table** - Growth vs limit per test
3. **Full output** - Last 50 lines for context

## Dependencies

Requires:

- git_safety_guard-39k (actual memory tests)

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-a52","title":"Fix bypass: quoted command word lets destructive git/rm evade rules","description":"## Background / Motivation

We discovered a **false-negative bypass** where destructive commands are incorrectly allowed when the *executed command word* is quoted.

Examples that must be denied:
- `"git" reset --hard`
- `"rm" -rf /etc`
- `"/usr/bin/git" reset --hard`
- `echo hi; "rm" -rf /etc`
- `sudo -u root "rm" -rf /etc`

This is especially dangerous because it bypasses the core safety guarantees and can enable real data/work loss.

## Root cause

Current destructive/safe regex patterns match on token sequences like `git\s+reset\s+--hard` and `rm\s+-rf ...`.

When the command word is quoted (e.g., `"git"`), the stream contains `git"` / `"git"` and the expected whitespace boundary no longer exists at the command word boundary, so the patterns do not match.

Existing normalization handled only commands starting with `/.../git` or `/.../rm` and did **not** dequote executed command tokens.

## Fix

Centralize the fix in normalization (not in per-pattern regex loosening) to avoid creating new false positives in data contexts.

Implement a conservative normalization pass that:
- tokenizes the command into word/separator tokens,
- finds the **segment command word** (first executed word after wrappers/env-assignments),
- removes *outer quotes* from that command-word token only when it is a single token (no whitespace / separators inside),
- preserves quoted arguments to avoid synthesizing dangerous substrings in data contexts.

Wrapper awareness (for locating the executed command word) includes:
- `sudo` with option + option-arg skipping (e.g., `-u root`)
- `env` options and env assignments
- `command -v/-V` recognized as query mode (not execution)

Separators treated as segment boundaries include `;`, `|`, `||`, `&`, `&&`, `(`, `)`.

## Tests

Add regression coverage:
- Unit tests for `normalize_command()`:
  - dequote executed command words for git/rm and quoted absolute paths
  - dequote after separators and wrappers/options
  - negative tests: do not dequote quoted arguments (e.g., `echo "rm" ...`)
  - negative tests: do not dequote for `command -v` query mode
- E2E tests in `scripts/e2e_test.sh`:
  - ensure the quoted forms above are **BLOCKED**

## Acceptance

- Previously-allowed bypass commands are now denied.
- No new false positives in common data contexts.
- `cargo fmt --check`, `cargo check --all-targets`, `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `cargo test`, `cargo build --release`, `./scripts/e2e_test.sh` all pass.

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-adw","title":"[CATEGORY] monitoring.* - Observability Pack Suite","description":"# Observability Pack Suite

## Overview
Monitoring and alerting systems are critical for detecting and responding to incidents. Deleting dashboards, alerts, or integrations can leave teams blind to production issues.

## Packs to Implement

### monitoring.datadog
- **Why Critical**: Datadog is widely used for APM, logs, and metrics. Dashboard/monitor deletion loses carefully tuned alerting.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `datadog-ci monitors delete`
  - `datadog-ci dashboards delete`
  - API calls with DELETE method
  - `terraform destroy` on Datadog resources
- **Safe Patterns**: `get`, `list`, `search`, `validate`

### monitoring.prometheus
- **Why Critical**: Prometheus/Grafana stack powers open-source observability. Rule deletion affects alerting.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - Deleting prometheus rules files
  - `promtool` rule removal
  - Grafana dashboard deletion via API
  - `kubectl delete` on monitoring namespace
- **Safe Patterns**: `promtool check`, `promtool query`

### monitoring.pagerduty
- **Why Critical**: PagerDuty routes critical alerts. Service/schedule deletion breaks incident response.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `pd service delete`
  - `pd schedule delete`
  - `pd escalation-policy delete`
  - API DELETE calls
- **Safe Patterns**: `list`, `get`, `show`

### monitoring.newrelic
- **Why Critical**: New Relic provides full-stack observability. Policy deletion affects alert routing.
- **Key Destructive Patterns**:
  - `newrelic entity delete`
  - `newrelic apm application delete`
  - GraphQL mutations for deletion
- **Safe Patterns**: `query`, `list`, `get`

## Dependencies
- Depends on: Tier 2 Epic (git_safety_guard-hhh)

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Major monitoring platforms covered

 {"id":"git_safety_guard-ahm","title":"BUG: Config precedence cannot override back to defaults","description":"## Problem

Config layering claims "other takes priority" (env > project > user > system > defaults), but current `Config::merge` uses sentinel/monotonic merges that prevent higher-priority layers from explicitly setting values back to defaults.

Examples observed in `src/config.rs` merge logic:
- `general.verbose` can only ever become `true`; cannot be set back to `false`.
- `general.color` cannot be set back to `auto`.
- `logging.enabled` can only ever become `true`; cannot be disabled.
- `logging.format` cannot be set back to `text`.
- `logging.events` filters are one-way toggles (deny/warn only disable; allow only enable).

This breaks the mental model of config precedence, makes behavior hard to reason about, and can cause persistent "can't turn it off" UX footguns that feel like false positives / velocity killers (especially verbose/logging output).

## Root cause

Config files deserialize into fully-defaulted structs (`#[serde(default)]`), losing information about whether a field was absent vs explicitly set to the default value. Merge currently approximates presence by comparing to default/sentinel values, which fails for "explicit default" cases.

## Goal

Make config layering **presence-aware** so that higher-precedence configs can explicitly set any supported value (including the default value) and have it win deterministically.

## Proposed approach

- Parse file configs into a "layer"/"partial" representation where scalar fields are `Option<T>` so we preserve field presence.
- Merge layers into the effective `Config` by applying only fields that are `Some(...)`.
- Keep existing additive semantics for list/map sections (packs/rules) unless explicitly re-scoped.

## Acceptance criteria

- If system config sets `general.verbose=true` and user config sets `general.verbose=false`, effective config is `false`.
- If system config sets `general.color="never"` and user config sets `general.color="auto"`, effective config is `auto`.
- If system config enables logging and user config disables it, effective config disables.
- Similar reversibility for `logging.format`, redaction, and event filters.

## Tests

Add unit tests that construct two TOML layers (system + user) and verify precedence for the above cases. Prefer tests that parse TOML into the new layer type to ensure real-world behavior.
